title: Children of Blood and Bone

passage.title: Children of Blood and Bone (excerpt)
passage.author: By Tomi Adeyemi
passage.text:
**This novel is set in the kingdom of Orïsha, where powerful magicians once lived and thrived. Years ago, King Saran banned magic and ordered the Raid, a deadly attack against those who practiced it. He used a metal called *majacite* to hurt them and take away their powers. This passage is narrated by Inan Olúbori, the prince of Orïsha and the son of King Saran, who has just discovered his own magical abilities.**


1. Out of instinct, I finger the [word] [word] in my pocket, a stolen “gift” from Father. The [word] piece is the only survivor of Father’s childhood set, a game of strategy he used to play with me when I was young. Though the cool metal usually anchors me, today it’s warm to the touch.[...]

2. “I hate my magic.” I lower my voice. “I [word] the way it poisons me. But more than anything, I hate the way it makes me hate myself.” It takes more strength than I have to lift my head and meet [word]’s gaze. Looking at her stirs up every single shame.

3. Zélie’s eyes water once more. I don’t know what chord I've struck. Her sea-salt soul seems to shrink away. For the first time, I want it to stay.

4. “Your magic isn’t poison.” Her voice shakes. “You are. You push it down, you fight it back. You carry around that pathetic toy.” She stomps over and rips the sênet pawn out of my hand, shoving it in my face. “This is *majacite*, you idiot. I’m surprised all your fingers haven’t fallen off.” 

5. I stare at the tarnished pawn, the gold and brown rust hiding its original color. I always thought the piece was painted black, but could it really have been made of *majacite* the whole time?

6. I take it from her hands, holding it gently, feeling the way it pricks my skin. All this time I thought I was just squeezing it too tight. *Of course…* I almost laugh at the irony. The realization brings me back to the moment I got it. The day Father “gifted” it to me. 

7. Before the Raid, we played sênet every week. An hour where Father became more than a king. Every piece and move was a lesson, wisdom for the day I would lead.

8. But after the Raid, there was no time for games. No time for me. One day I made the mistake of carrying the game into the throne room and Father threw the pieces in my face. 

9. *Leave it*, he barked when I bent down to pick them up. *Servants clean. Kings don’t.*

10. This pawn was the only piece I managed to [word]. Shame ripples through me as I stare at the tarnished metal. The only gift he’s ever given me, and at its core is hate.

11. “This belonged to my father,” I speak quietly. A secret weapon taken from others who despised magic. Created to destroy others like me. 

12. “You clutch it the way a child clutches a blanket.” Zélie releases a heavy sigh. “You fight for a man who will always hate you just because of what you are.” Like her hair, her silver gaze glows in the moonlight, more piercing than any eyes that have ever seen through me. I stare.

13. I stare though I need to talk. 

14. I drop the pawn in the dirt and kick it aside. I must draw a line in the sand. I’ve been a sheep. A sheep when my kingdom needed me to act like a king.

15. *Duty before self.* 

16. The [word] unravels before my eyes, taking my Father’s lies with it. Magic may be dangerous but the sins of [word] it have made the monarchy no better.

17. “I know you can’t trust me, but give me this chance to prove myself. I’ll get us into that camp. I’ll bring your brother back.”

18. Zélie bites her lip. “And when we find the scroll?” 

19. I hesitate; Father’s face flashes in my mind. *If we don’t stop magic, all of Orïsha will burn.* 

20. But the only fires I’ve seen have been by his hand. His and mine. I’ve given him a lifetime. I can’t [word] any more of his lies.

21. “It’s yours,” I decide. “Whatever you and Amari are trying to do…I won’t stand in your way.”

22. I hold out my hand and she stares at it; I don’t know if my words are enough. But after a long moment, she places her palm in mine.

questions.question_content: **Which inference is best supported by the text?**

choices.label: A
choices.content: Zélie and Inan’s father have different views about magic.

choices.label: B
choices.content: Inan and his father used to play sênet together.

choices.label: C
choices.content: Inan’s father knows about Inan’s magic and accepts him.

choices.label: D
choices.content: Zélie and Inan are best friends, even though she doesn’t like his father.

note: KA serves 1/4 questions to anonymous users. Passage and sample question captured for skeleton generation.
